caveats: a: PECO is a large rental network, not a representative LMM manufacturer.
a: Vendor-reported capabilities and performance require independent verification.
a: Robotics and conveyance are complementary capital equipment, not AI substitution alone.

rho: Census adoption data cover all businesses rather than this industry.
rho: The 2024 injury rate of 3.9 cases per 100 full-time workers increases the implementation burden.

e: Adjacent pooling evidence may not represent establishments classified as manufacturers.
e: The supplied LMM count is a margin-bridged estimate based on 2022 receipts and a January 2026 sector margin.

s5: The survey category includes gifts and rare IPOs that may not meet the screen.

d5: Reusable pallets reduce new-unit demand even when pallet trips grow.
d5: Demand varies with inventories, consumer goods, industrial production, and freight cycles.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Eligible firms earn repeat B2B revenue from customer-specific pallets, crates, boxes, and related managed supply, often priced per unit or program and supplemented by inspection, repair coordination, inventory, or service commitments.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off commodity pallet or box sales without a recurring outsourced relationship, pure pallet rental or pooling companies not classified in the industry, repair-only firms with no manufacturing activity, captive packaging shops, brokers, software vendors, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market wood pallet, box, crate, and container manufacturers that repeatedly provide outsourced specification, production, inspection, repair-support, inventory, or managed-supply services to external industrial, retail, food, logistics, and distribution customers.

a.bridge: Pallet-specific vendor and operator evidence demonstrates inspection, grading, routing, production tracking, and repair-instruction capabilities, but it does not measure the wage-weighted not-yet-automated task share across LMM NAICS 321920 firms and is weighted toward pallet rental and repair networks. This may overstate applicability to small custom-crate shops, while understating production planning and administrative opportunities.
caveats: PECO is a large rental network, not a representative LMM manufacturer.
Vendor-reported capabilities and performance require independent verification.
Robotics and conveyance are complementary capital equipment, not AI substitution alone.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task and automation census across 20-30 eligible LMM plants, measuring time in cutting, nailing, assembly, inspection, grading, repair, handling, forklift work, inventory counts, scheduling, quoting, dispatch, and customer reporting, segmented by new versus recycled pallets and custom containers.
a.rationale: Pallet and container work combines cutting, nailing, assembly, lifting, sorting, inspection, repair, inventory, scheduling, dispatch, and customer administration. PECO reports a vision system reducing full pallet inspection from up to one minute manually to six seconds and generating repair instructions and routing. Current systems also monitor throughput, defects, labor, safety, inventory, and grading. Physical cutting, assembly, repair, and material movement remain less directly exposed to AI without robotics.

d5.bridge: Brambles pallet-pooling volume and contract wins proxy for recurring demand for pallets and related services, while BLS woodworker projections proxy for labor demand rather than constant-quality service quantity. Pooling may gain share from whitewood manufacturers, so its growth can overstate total 321920 demand but understate demand for the manufacturing and repair inputs needed to support pooled fleets.
caveats: Pooling volumes are global or regional and not equivalent to US manufacturing quantity.
Reusable pallets reduce new-unit demand even when pallet trips grow.
Demand varies with inventories, consumer goods, industrial production, and freight cycles.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year US unit forecasts from NWPCA, Freedonia, Reusable Packaging Association, freight and industrial-production data, customer automation plans, pallet-pooling penetration, recycled-pallet turns, and eligible-firm order volumes separated by new, repaired, custom, and pooled pallets.
d5.rationale: Pallets and containers are embedded in grocery, consumer goods, industrial production, warehousing, retail, and freight. Brambles reported 2% Americas pallet volume growth from new customers in FY2025 and said higher automation in customer supply chains and limited quality-whitewood availability supported SME conversion to pooling, while like-for-like US volume declined 1% amid weak conditions. Broad woodworker employment is projected down 2% through 2034, consistent with productivity rather than disappearance of physical demand. A modest-growth base with macro-sensitive downside is appropriate.

caveats: NAICS does not separate recurring managed supply from one-off product sales.
Adjacent pooling evidence may not represent establishments classified as manufacturers.
The supplied LMM count is a margin-bridged estimate based on 2022 receipts and a January 2026 sector margin.
e.change_evidence: Classify the supplied 647-firm LMM estimate using revenue by standard spot sales, custom engineered packaging, recurring customer programs, repair and retrieval, inventory management, just-in-time delivery, tracking, rental or pooling, and pure manufacturing.
e.rationale: The NAICS code is defined by manufactured pallets and containers, so commodity unit sales alone do not qualify. A meaningful subset repeatedly designs and supplies customer-specific pallets, crates, or boxes and bundles inventory, inspection, quality, repair coordination, or managed programs. Large pooling networks demonstrate that customers value recurring pallet service, but pure poolers are excluded unless the target firm itself remains inside 321920.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $3,803.1M / sectoral output (T30) $15,397.6M, year 2023

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $285,757 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 321920); class <5: 805 firms, avg revenue/firm $779,755 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $114,234 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 444 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,855,232 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $271,791 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 446 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,551,666 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $520,319 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 545 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,294,092 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,508,084 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 102 firms, avg revenue/firm $36,435,078 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,337,739 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $213,370,545 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $31,258,785 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 647

caveats: Pooling can shift ownership and service provision without eliminating operator-required demand.
Substitution differs sharply between standardized pallets and engineered export crates.
No direct customer survey measures future self-supply or software-only substitution.
o.change_evidence: Interviews with large manufacturers, grocers, retailers, 3PLs, exporters, and automation integrators on five-year plans for plastic or corrugated substitution, pallet pooling, captive repair, direct sourcing, autonomous inspection, and packaging redesign.
o.rationale: A pallet, crate, or wood container is a physical load-bearing platform that must be manufactured, inspected, repaired, and supplied with accountable quality and safety. Software can track assets, plan networks, and automate inspection but cannot replace the physical operator. Plastic, corrugated, metal, reusable pooling, customer-owned automation, and longer pallet life can shift demand away from the current kind of firm, leaving a small reduction from one.

q.bridge: Brambles reports pricing and cost recovery in global pallet pooling contracts, not five-year retention of AI benefits by US LMM wood pallet manufacturers. Contractual indexation likely overstates automatic pass-through for smaller manufacturers, while pooling's network differentiation may overstate retention. The evidence is used to bound how recurring pallet customers and contracts transmit cost changes.
caveats: Brambles is a global pooling operator rather than an LMM manufacturer.
No source directly measures retention of implemented AI benefit.
Commodity pallets have weaker capture than engineered crates and managed programs.
q.change_evidence: Eligible-firm contract and bid review covering lumber and fuel indices, surcharge formulas, open-book provisions, annual resets, quality penalties, repair ownership, pooling or inventory fees, customer concentration, and margin persistence after automation deployments.
q.rationale: Brambles reports contractual repricing and indexation that recover cost-to-serve increases, including 1-2% price realization in major regions. This indicates customers ultimately absorb cost changes but also that savings can be reset through the same mechanisms. Standard pallets and competitive bids expose direct labor savings, while better quality, fewer claims, yield, uptime, faster turns, inventory visibility, and reliable delivery support retention in managed programs.

caveats: The strongest implementation claims come from vendors and a company press release.
Census adoption data cover all businesses rather than this industry.
The 2024 injury rate of 3.9 cases per 100 full-time workers increases the implementation burden.
rho.change_evidence: Independent deployment cohorts reporting installed cost, time to stable production, model accuracy by defect and pallet type, uptime, false reject rates, labor minutes per pallet, throughput, repair yield, injuries, and sustained staffing changes over 12-24 months.
rho.rationale: Standard pallet geometries, repetitive flows, visible defects, and high throughput make inspection, counting, grading, routing, safety monitoring, and repair instruction unusually implementable. Deployed North American systems support operational feasibility. Five-year realization is still limited by fragmented LMM plants, legacy equipment, dirty and variable wood, mixed pallet types, integration cost, worker safety, and the fact that assembly and handling require robotics or conveyance.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or ownership-transfer plans among US small-business owners across industries, not completed qualifying control transfers among LMM wood pallet and container manufacturers. The industry likely has many mature private owners, but completion failure, family gifts, and businesses dependent on a few customers or the owner reduce qualifying transfer probability.
caveats: Stated intent is not a completed transaction.
The source spans all industries and small-business sizes.
The survey category includes gifts and rare IPOs that may not meet the screen.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age, succession, customer-concentration, real-estate, and key-person interviews for eligible firms plus five years of closed transactions from PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, Axial, NWPCA contacts, equipment lenders, brokers, and state filings.
s5.rationale: Gallup found 14% of working business owners planned a sale, IPO, or ownership transfer within five years and 17% among owners age 55 or older. A 17% base reflects likely mature ownership across a large, fragmented industrial population, with a wide range for unobserved owner age, transferability, and execution.

principal_driver: Deployable AI inspection, grading, routing, repair, and operating visibility applied to high-volume recurring pallet programs.
principal_weakness: Commodity unit pricing and contractual cost recovery can pass visible productivity gains to customers, while many firms remain product sellers outside the service lens.
